title: Number of one-year-olds who are not vaccinated against measles
caption:
Number of one-year-olds who are not vaccinated against measles, 2023

Note: Measles is a highly contagious viral disease, most common in young children. Its effects include blindness, inflammation of the brain, severe diarrhea, and severe respiratory infections such as pneumonia.

The choropleth map displays the absolute number of one-year-olds who missed measles vaccination in 2023, using a continuous color scale ranging from light beige for zero unvaccinated children to deep maroon for one million. The highest concentrations of unvaccinated children appear in South Asia and parts of sub-Saharan Africa, where India, Nigeria, and Pakistan each fall into the darkest band representing one million unvaccinated infants. Ethiopia and the Democratic Republic of the Congo also show very high numbers, visible in the 300,000 to 1 million range. In contrast, most of Western Europe, Australia, Canada, and Japan sit in the lightest bands, indicating fewer than 10,000 unvaccinated one-year-olds. The United States and Brazil occupy mid-range shades, corresponding to between 30,000 and 100,000 unvaccinated children. Russia and China appear in lighter orange tones, suggesting figures below 100,000. Several countries in central Africa and Southeast Asia show intermediate values, clustered around the 30,000 to 100,000 threshold. Greenland and a few small island nations are marked as having no data. The geographic distribution shows that the burden of missed measles vaccinations is heavily concentrated in a small number of populous countries, while many high-income nations report minimal numbers. The pattern visible across continents places the largest absolute gaps in immunization coverage within specific regions of Asia and Africa.
